key_points: Cornell Courts praised as most spacious on-campus option
Lakeview and Westview waitlists fill fast; plan early
Some dorms close during breaks, forcing students out
key_quotes: Cornell; without a doubt. Even though the buildings are on the older side, they are very spacious, even more than both Lakeview and Westview.
If your hall says it's closed during breaks then you have to be out of your dorm the day break starts.
I lived in Sellers. The memories, I miss campus.

key_points: Campus goes dead Thursday night — it's a commuter school
Weekend activities require going off-campus to Ypsi or Ann Arbor
Arts and crafts types will struggle; partiers will be bored too
key_quotes: Eastern is a commuting school. Folks disappear for the weekend starting Thursday night.
Yes she will likely find herself bored, its a commuter school with offers less than WMU in terms of social activities.
Campus is pretty sleepy on the weekends. She'll want to venture off campus to find things to do.

key_points: Food quality is declining — undercooked food reported repeatedly
Recent dining system changes cut hours and reduced access points
Nothing open past 3pm under new system; lines expected to worsen
key_quotes: I refuse to eat there anymore after I kept getting undercooked everything.
Wow, disappointed to see the lack of dining options in the student center, and the lack of hours as well. Nothing open past 3pm? That's crazy.
I don't think anyone really asked for these changes. Its pretty disappointing.

key_points: DRC accommodations reportedly denied or ignored by professors
Autistic students specifically warned the HHS program is not accommodating
No strong positive mental health support data found in student posts
key_quotes: Depending program, NOT autism friendly. HHS program, but not social work. They continually denied ALL of my DRC accommodations.
I had many (psychology) professors who refused to follow my APPROVED accommodations.
So honestly I tell people not to go there in general.

key_points: Generous scholarships attract students, including full tuition offers
Strong value for international students with dedicated scholarship programs
Presidential scholarship is competitive and life-changing but rejection emails arrive fast
key_quotes: I was accepted in December with a pretty nice scholarship ($7500/yr for 4 years). Since I like money, EMU is one of my top choices.
EMU has offered me full tuition for 4 years.
My rejection email just came in. Congratulations to those who are moving on!!

key_points: Education and teacher prep programs are historically strong — EMU's founding mission
Small class sizes mean real faculty access and strong recommendation letters
CS program lacks ABET accreditation; graduation rate concerns raised repeatedly
key_quotes: Eastern was created as a teacher's college in 1849. Their programs cater to students who want to be teachers.
Yes because you can really work with and get to know the faculty, which will lead to excellent letters of recommendation. Much more difficult at a bigger school.
Here's my problem with EMU. First, as you said, there is a 13 percent graduation rate. This is not a red flag to me so much as it a sign that reads 'DO NOT ENTER'.

key_points: Parking system is a widely-mocked administrative failure
Dining changes made without student input, causing real accessibility harm
ICE activity on campus with a delayed or contradictory university response
key_quotes: I'm literally in the system enough for you to take my money, but not enough for you to not give me tickets. Make it make sense.
Yeah they just love fucking shit up.
I received an email from university communications saying no activity was reported. That email must have been right before they came. So horrible.

key_points: Ypsilanti has a real arts scene; Ann Arbor is accessible nearby
Campus has friendly fox squirrels, a tropical greenhouse, and mapped trees
Ballard St free parking described as 'sort of sketchy' with people sleeping there
key_quotes: Ypsi has a huge arts scene that should be very easy for her to get into. EMU itself doesn't have a whole lot going on, but the surrounding area does.
Get your coffee from Sweetwaters then take it to the greenhouse on campus. There's a study table in the 3rd room, and it's an AWESOME tropical greenhouse.
Ballard st Parking Lot. About 3-4 min walk to campus. Sort of sketchy tho. Got there one morning to see people sleeping in a mattress in the parking lot.

key_points: CS grads report median salary of $78k — decent for the price
Education and HR/MBA grads report solid career outcomes
Weaker graduate school pipeline compared to larger research universities
key_quotes: EMU has a median salary of 78K for CS graduates, which is not a bad deal if they are so affordable.
I got my MBA from EMU and I think it was one of the best investments I've made. Really opened up my career path.
The school also has no grad school for CS... I wouldn't want to stay there for grad school.

key_points: Full-tuition scholarships make EMU a serious option over more expensive schools
Parking fees ($220-$250/semester) add cost with broken enforcement systems
Low graduation rate undercuts the value proposition for underprepared students
key_quotes: EMU has offered me full tuition for 4 years. But the problem is, EMU doesn't seem to be a good school, the statistics I've looked at say only 10% of people there graduate in 4 years.
Oh my god they're $220 a semester now??!?! I eventually just stopped paying that extortion racket and started parking off campus.
My life is 10 times better now. I went on to a successful career in Information systems. No more dead end jobs!

key_points: Students who thrive here are self-directed and don't rely on campus energy
Tight-knit pockets exist but you have to seek them out actively
Alumni sentiment is warm but honest — 'the experience is what you make it'
key_quotes: Find your place, or make one if you can't. The experience will be as great/terrible as you make it, if that makes sense.
As an Eastern alum, that place turned me into a man. I will cherish my time at Eastern forever!
I lived in the Ann Arbor student co-ops when I was a student at EMU — campus wasn't where the action was for me.

key_points: **Graduation Rate:** Only ~10-13% graduate in 4 years — students and commenters call it a dealbreaker
**Disability Services:** DRC accommodations denied or ignored; autistic students explicitly warned away
**Parking:** Paid $220-$250 passes don't register in system; students ticketed repeatedly with no recourse
key_quotes: This is not a red flag to me so much as it a sign that reads 'DO NOT ENTER'. The school also has no grad school for CS.
They continually denied ALL of my DRC accommodations. So honestly I tell people not to go there in general.
I'm literally in the system enough for you to take my money, but not enough for you to not give me tickets.

key_points: Campus greenhouse has a tropical study room — almost no one knows about it
Geology and natural science faculty offer unusually close mentorship relationships
Ypsilanti's downtown arts scene (Riverside Arts Center, Dzanc House) is walkable and underrated
key_quotes: Get your coffee from Sweetwaters then take it to the greenhouse on campus. There's a study table in the 3rd room, and it's an AWESOME tropical greenhouse.
Yes because you can really work with and get to know the faculty, which will lead to excellent letters of recommendation. Much more difficult at a bigger school.
Ypsi has a huge arts scene that should be very easy for her to get into. Check out the Riverside Arts Center and Dzanc House in downtown Ypsi.
